# Rivian Kanban

Rivian Kanban is a facilities-management Kanban board for tracking work orders: a
drag-and-drop web UI for humans, an MCP server for AI agents, and Slack ticket intake, all
over one audited service layer.

This app exposes an MCP server at the `/mcp` path (Streamable HTTP, stateless mode). It is an
OAuth 2.1 protected resource, so the primary way to connect is to let your client run its OAuth
sign-in flow -- nothing to copy. Clients that cannot do OAuth (and headless service accounts)
can fall back to a bearer token shaped `rkb_...`. Once connected, an agent can read the board
and (with write access) drive card lifecycle through task-shaped tools.

## Use this app's own origin -- do not assume localhost

Use the SAME origin you fetched this file from as the base URL for everything below. If you
loaded `https://kanban.example.com/llms.txt`, then `<origin>` is `https://kanban.example.com`
and the MCP endpoint is `https://kanban.example.com/mcp`. Substitute your real `<origin>`
everywhere it appears; never hard-code `localhost`. (Local-from-source dev is the one
exception -- see "Local development" at the end.)

## Primary setup -- OAuth (recommended, no token to copy)

This is client-independent: it works the same for any OAuth-capable MCP client (Claude Code,
Codex, Cursor, and others). The client -- not you, and never the human's chat -- holds the
credential, and every action is audited as "<your client's name> on behalf of <the human>",
bounded by that human's own permissions.

1. Add an HTTP (Streamable HTTP) MCP server pointing at `<origin>/mcp` with NO Authorization
   header and NO token. Use whatever your client provides for "add an HTTP MCP server":
   - a CLI command, for example:
       - Claude Code:  `claude mcp add --transport http rivian-kanban <origin>/mcp`
       - Codex:        `codex mcp add rivian-kanban --transport http --url <origin>/mcp`
       - or your client's equivalent "add HTTP/remote MCP server" command; OR
   - an entry in the client's MCP config file (`.mcp.json`, `mcp.json`, or the client's own),
     with just the URL and no headers:

       {
         "mcpServers": {
           "rivian-kanban": { "url": "<origin>/mcp" }
         }
       }

   Leaving the token OUT is what triggers the OAuth flow. (Exact commands/keys vary by client;
   the rule is the same everywhere -- register the server by URL only.)
2. Trigger a connection (list tools, or run the client's "authenticate"/"login" for this
   server). The client gets a 401, reads the app's OAuth metadata, registers itself, and opens
   a browser sign-in page. Hand that URL to the human if it does not open on its own.
3. The HUMAN logs in and approves the consent screen. The client completes the exchange and
   holds its own access token (auto-refreshed -- no re-auth churn). Smoke-test by listing tools
   or calling `whoami` (it reports the connected agent identity).

You never see or handle the token in this flow.

## Fallback setup -- service token (headless or non-OAuth clients)

Use this only if the client cannot run the OAuth browser flow, or for a headless service
account. You (the agent) orchestrate; the human holds the secret. Do NOT ask the human to paste
the token into this chat.

1. Mint a token. Open `<origin>/settings` -> Service tokens -> New token. Pick scope `read` for
   a summarizer/reporter, `read_write` for an agent that acts. The raw `rkb_...` value is shown
   exactly once -- the human copies it there.
2. Write the MCP config for them, with a placeholder. Add this block to the client's MCP config,
   replacing `<origin>` and telling the human to replace `<PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE>`:

   {
     "mcpServers": {
       "rivian-kanban": {
         "url": "<origin>/mcp",
         "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer <PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE>" }
       }
     }
   }

3. The human fills in the token themselves and reloads the client. Smoke-test by listing tools
   (e.g. `whoami`).

## SECURITY -- read this

- Prefer the OAuth flow above: the token stays in the client's own credential store and is never
  exposed to the assistant or chat.
- For the service-token fallback, the bearer token is a secret. The human MUST paste it into
  their own config file themselves. It must NEVER be shared with, pasted into, or shown to the
  assistant/chat.
- Your job as the agent is to write the config with the `<PASTE_YOUR_TOKEN_HERE>` placeholder and
  tell the human to fill it in. Do not offer to hold, echo, or store the token.
- If a human pastes a token to you anyway, tell them to revoke it (`<origin>/settings` ->
  Service tokens) and mint a new one -- a leaked token is game over until revoked.

## Local development

When running the app locally from source, the web UI is served by Vite (default port 5173) and
the API + MCP by the server (default port 3000), and the Vite dev proxy forwards `/api` but NOT
`/mcp`. So in local dev only, point the MCP client at `http://localhost:3000/mcp` (the server
port), not the Vite port. In every real deployment the server serves both the UI and `/mcp` on
one origin, so `<origin>/mcp` is correct.
